Investment holding steady with no acceleration or deceleration.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200064; PV 10.5%","signal_text":"GST collections at 200064 YoY with PV sales at 10.5% signals demand surge. Consumer spending broad-based across durables and non-durables.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% and PMI composite at 56.5 look unambiguous — until you notice that PMI has decelerated from 58.9 prior month, core sector growth has collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7%, and capacity utilisation has slipped to 74.8%. Mishra's triangulation test: IIP at 5.2% is respectable, but the capital goods surge to +12.5% is a single-month spike from 4.3% — not a trend. Invert the headline: if core sector (which covers 40% of IIP) is decelerating sharply while capital goods surges, the composition of growth is narrowing, not broadening.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% is benign on the surface — but food inflation has jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% prior month, a 134bps acceleration in a single print, while core CPI sits at 3.4%. WPI at 2.13% (up from 1.81%) and the FAO food index at 128.5 (up from 125.5) suggest pipeline pressure is building — fuel deflation at -3.78% is the only reason headline stays below 3.5%. Munger's inversion: RBI is cutting rates into a food inflation acceleration and an oil price at the 82nd percentile — if Brent sustains above $95, the fuel deflation cushion evaporates within two quarters and CPI prints above 4.5%, forcing a policy reversal that markets are not pricing.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 14.3%%; CD 83.04%%","signal_text":"Bank credit at +14.3% YoY against deposit growth of +10.8% — a 350bps divergence widening for three consecutive months — has pushed the CD ratio to 83.04%, up from 82.38%. NBFC credit growth moderating to 15-17% from 18-20% signals that wholesale funding stress is already transmitting; corporate bond issuance at ₹85,000+ crore versus ₹60,000 crore in FY22-23 means banks are losing deposit share to capital markets precisely when they need deposits most. Historical precedent: the last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during an easing cycle was Q2 FY14 — within 12 months, the taper tantrum forced 75bps of emergency hikes; today's FX reserves are $100bn higher, but FII outflows at -₹8,692 crore accelerating 2.6x month-on-month suggest the carry-trade unwind has already begun.","badge_label":"Stable Credit","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% — down from 5.50% — places the real policy rate at approximately 200bps above CPI, which is accommodative in name but restrictive in transmission given the CD ratio constraint. For housing, the average home loan rate at 8.15% (down from 8.4%) is improving affordability — the affordability index has moved to 0.61 from 0.46 — but the RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised sharply to 4.6% from 2.1%, a 250bps upward revision that signals internal discomfort with the easing trajectory. Munger's incentive check: RBI is cutting while its own forecast implies real rates will compress to near zero — the institution is betting that oil stays benign and food normalises; Brent at $98.66 is a direct challenge to that bet.","badge_label":"Easing Cycle","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU 74.8%%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at +12.5% — an 820bps acceleration from 4.3% — is the most eye-catching number in today's data set, and almost certainly a trap. Capacity utilisation at 74.8% — down from 75.8% and 300-500bps below the 78-80% threshold that historically triggers broad private capex cycles — means corporates have no rational incentive to add capacity. Core sector at 2.3% (covering cement, steel, electricity — the physical proxies for actual investment) contradicts the capital goods IIP entirely; one of these numbers is wrong, and Mishra's rule is to trust the physical proxy over the survey-based index.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200064; PV 13%","signal_text":"GST collections breaching ₹2.0 lakh crore for the first time is structurally significant — but Mishra's formalization lens demands the question: how much is genuine demand expansion versus GST-net widening as informal activity formalises? Passenger vehicle sales at +13% and two-wheeler at +13.4% are both strong, and the two-wheeler print matters more — it is the rural and semi-urban demand signal that GDP aggregates miss. E-commerce GMV growth decelerating to 12.4% from 25-30% in FY25 is the counter-signal: urban discretionary is plateauing even as rural durables recover, a divergence that historically precedes a consumption mix shift rather than a broad acceleration.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}

signal_cards.2026-04-08:1: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":1,"signal_theme":"CREDIT CYCLE","status":"risk","title":"Deposit Gap Widens—Funding Stress Ahead","data_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% (prev: 82.38%), bank credit growth at 14.3% vs. deposit growth at 10.9%. Credit-to-deposit gap widening materially. NBFC credit growth holding at 15-17% despite deposit constraints.","implication":"Banks and NBFCs face structural funding pressure. Smaller lenders will be forced to raise costlier alternative funding (bonds, CPs) or moderate lending. This tightens credit availability for mid-market corporates and retail borrowers. Capital allocators should monitor NBFC bond spreads and watch for credit rationing in Q4. Duration plays face headwind if funding costs rise.","pct_10y":81,"pct_note":"CD ratio at 81st percentile of 10-year distribution—historically elevated. Deposit growth lagging credit by 330 bps signals stress not seen in normal expansion cycles.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:2: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":2,"signal_theme":"CAPEX TRIGGER","status":"watch","title":"Capex Steady but No Acceleration Signal","data_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY (prev: 4.3%), capacity utilisation at 74.80% (prev: 75.80%). Core sector growth slowed to 2.3% YoY (prev: 4.7%). RE launches at 126.265 units (prev: 123.835), office absorption strong at 83.3 (prev: 73).","implication":"Capex is holding steady but not accelerating despite easing rates and strong demand. Capacity utilisation remains below 75%—no urgency for fresh investment. Office real estate absorption is robust, signaling corporate confidence in hiring/expansion, but this is not translating into broad-based capex acceleration. Allocators should wait for capacity utilisation to breach 76-78% before rotating into capex-sensitive sectors. Current momentum is demand-driven, not investment-driven.","pct_10y":50,"pct_note":"IIP capgoods volatility is high; 12.5% is mid-cycle. Core sector deceleration to 2.3% suggests capex impulse is fading despite strong PMI.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:3: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":3,"signal_theme":"SIP / RETAIL FLOWS","status":"positive","title":"Retail Equity Flows Resilient Despite Volatility","data_text":"SIP inflows at ₹29,845 Cr (prev: ₹31,002 Cr, -3.7% MoM), SIP YoY growth at 15%, SIP accounts at 9.92 Cr (prev: 9.79 Cr), SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr (prev: ₹16.36 Tr). Equity MF net inflows at ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr). MF AUM at ₹82.03 Tr (prev: ₹80.24 Tr). NFO collections surged to ₹3,955 Cr (prev: ₹806 Cr).","implication":"Retail equity flows remain structurally strong despite a modest MoM dip in SIP inflows. Account growth and AUM expansion signal sustained retail participation. NFO collections 4.9x higher MoM indicate fresh retail capital entering markets. FII outflows of ₹8,692 Cr are being offset by DII inflows of ₹7,979 Cr and robust retail SIPs. This is a structural floor under equity markets. Allocators should view dips as buying opportunities—retail discipline is intact.","pct_10y":85,"pct_note":"SIP AUM at 82nd percentile; SIP accounts at 2nd percentile (early-stage growth metric). Combined, retail flows are at multi-year highs in absolute terms.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:4: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":4,"signal_theme":"OIL / COMMODITY RISK","status":"positive","title":"Oil Retreat Eases Inflation, Copper Signals Growth","data_text":"Brent crude at $94.98/bbl (prev: $109.27), WTI at $95.10 (prev: $112.95). Fuel inflation at -3.78% (prev: -4.01%). Copper at $5.766/lb (prev: $5.544), at 95th percentile of 10-year range. Iron ore at $161.91/t (prev: $108.32). Natural gas at $2.706 (prev: $2.87).","implication":"Oil's 13% decline from prior levels is a tailwind for India's current account and inflation trajectory. Fuel deflation is supporting RBI's easing cycle. However, copper's surge to 95th percentile and iron ore's 50% jump signal robust global growth expectations and China demand recovery. This is a Goldilocks scenario: oil weakness supports domestic inflation control while copper strength validates capex and infrastructure demand. Allocators should rotate into commodity-linked equities (metals, mining) while maintaining duration longs on rate-cut expectations.","pct_10y":67,"pct_note":"~Brent at 67th percentile; copper at 95th percentile. Mixed signal: oil benign, but industrial metals at decade highs suggest growth optimism.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:5: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":5,"signal_theme":"GLOBAL LIQUIDITY","status":"positive","title":"Global Risk-On: Equities Surge, Volatility Collapses","data_text":"S&P 500 at 6,779.18 (prev: 6,611.83, +2.5%), Nasdaq at 22,652.87 (prev: 21,996.34, +3.0%), at 100th percentile. Nikkei225 at 56,308.42 (prev: 53,429.56, +5.4%), at 96th percentile. Euro Stoxx50 at 5,913.37 (prev: 5,633.22, +5.0%), at 98th percentile. US VIX at 21.25 (prev: 25.78), India VIX at 19.698 (prev: 24.7). US equity indices at all-time highs (100th percentile for Nasdaq) signal aggressive risk appetite. Dollar weakness (DXY down) and falling US yields are fueling EM inflows. India's VIX compression to 19.7 reflects this global liquidity wave. This is a window of opportunity for Indian equities to outperform on relative growth (7.8% GDP vs. US 0.7% SAAR). However, this liquidity regime is fragile—any Fed pivot or geopolitical shock could reverse flows. Allocators should use this strength to rebalance, not chase.","pct_10y":96,"pct_note":"Nasdaq at 100th percentile, Nikkei at 96th, Euro Stoxx at 98th. Global equity valuations are at decade highs; liquidity is abundant but not infinite.","is_surprise":false}

signal_cards.2026-04-08:7: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Real Estate Affordability Crisis Masks Demand Surge","data_text":"Affordability index at 0.61 (prev: 0.46, +32% deterioration). Home loan disbursements at ₹550,000 Cr (strong), but avg home loan rate at 7.65% (prev: 8.15%, -50 bps). RE launches at 126.265 units (prev: 123.835), RE sales at 101.675 units (prev: 108.97, -6.7% decline). Unsold inventory at 600,000 units. Office absorption at 83.3 (prev: 73, +13.7%), office vacancy at 15.2% (prev: 15.7%). Embassy REIT at ₹429.85, Mindspace REIT at ₹449.98 (both at 75-79th percentiles).","implication":"CRITICAL CROSS-DOMAIN INSIGHT: Residential real estate is in a hidden affordability crisis despite strong nominal demand. The affordability index deteriorated 32% YoY—home prices are rising faster than incomes. Launches are up but sales are down 6.7%, and unsold inventory sits at 600,000 units. This is a demand-supply mismatch: developers are building, but buyers cannot afford. However, COMMERCIAL real estate (offices) is booming—absorption up 13.7%, vacancy falling, REITs at decade highs. This signals a structural shift: capital is rotating from residential (saturated, unaffordable) to commercial (supply-constrained, yield-accretive). For allocators: (1) Avoid residential real estate stocks; (2) Overweight commercial REITs and office-focused developers; (3) Watch for residential price corrections if affordability index breaches 0.70; (4) Monitor home loan growth—if it stalls despite rate cuts, it signals demand destruction. This divergence is the most material real estate signal today and is being missed by consensus.","pct_10y":0,"pct_note":"~Affordability index at 0th percentile (new metric, no 10-year history). Office absorption at 82nd percentile. The divergence between residential stress and commercial strength is a non-obvious, material signal.","is_surprise":true}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:1: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":1,"signal_theme":"CREDIT CYCLE","status":"watch","title":"Deposit Gap Widens — RBI's Easing Trap","data_text":"Bank credit +14.3% YoY vs deposits +10.8% YoY (Feb 2026) — a 350bps divergence that has widened 3 consecutive months. CD ratio at 83.04% (up from 82.38% prior month). NBFC credit growth moderating to 15-17% from 18-20% average, signalling wholesale funding stress. Corporate bond issuance holding at 85,000+ crore (FY25-26) vs 60,000 crore in FY22-23 — banks are losing deposit share to capital markets.","implication":"RBI's easing cycle (repo at 5.25%, down 25bps) is masking a structural deposit shortage. Banks with CASA ratios below 40% will face margin compression as they chase deposits at higher rates. Overweight private banks with CASA > 45% (HDFC, ICICI, Axis) — they'll poach deposits from weaker peers. Avoid NBFC exposure with wholesale funding > 30% of liabilities; they're the first to face funding stress in a liquidity tightening. Duration plays are crowded; rotate into bank equity.","pct_10y":83,"pct_note":"CD ratio at 83% is at the 83rd percentile — historically, ratios above 85% have preceded RBI liquidity tightening and margin compression for smaller banks. This is late-cycle credit behaviour.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:2: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":2,"signal_theme":"CAPEX TRIGGER","status":"positive","title":"Capital Goods Surge Masks Capacity Slack","data_text":"IIP capital goods +12.5% YoY (latest) vs +4.3% prior month — a 820bps acceleration. However, capacity utilisation at 74.8% (down from 75.8% prior month) remains below the 78-80% threshold that typically triggers broad capex cycles. Core sector growth has decelerated sharply to +2.3% YoY from +4.7% prior month — a 240bps drop. GST collections at ₹2.0 lakh crore YoY signal broad-based demand, but capex is not yet self-sustaining.","implication":"The capital goods spike is likely driven by government infrastructure spending and replacement cycles, not organic private capex acceleration. Capacity utilisation at 74.8% leaves 5-6% headroom before capex becomes urgent — we're 2-3 quarters away from a genuine capex trigger. Avoid broad-based capex plays (Larsen & Toubro, Cummins) until capacity utilisation breaches 77%. Overweight government-linked capex beneficiaries (NTPC, Power Grid, REC) and selective infra plays with order visibility. Core sector deceleration is a yellow flag — monitor cement, steel, and power closely.","pct_10y":64,"pct_note":"IIP capital goods at the 64th percentile historically; the recent spike is cyclical, not structural. Capacity utilisation at 74.8% is below the 78-80% sweet spot that has preceded sustained capex cycles in the past 10 years.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:3: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":3,"signal_theme":"SIP / RETAIL FLOWS","status":"watch","title":"SIP Growth Decelerating — Affluent Money Entering","data_text":"SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore (Feb 2026) down from ₹31,002 crore prior month. SIP YoY growth at 15% (down from 17% prior month). However, SIP accounts +1.6 crore (104.5M from 102.9M) while SIP AUM +₹16.64 lakh crore (up from ₹16.36 lakh crore) — average ticket size rising to ₹15,900 per account (up from ₹15,880). Equity MF net inflows at ₹25,977 crore (up from ₹24,028 crore), but NFO collections at ₹1,939 crore (6th percentile) signal weak new investor acquisition.","implication":"Mass retail SIP growth is decelerating (15% YoY), but average ticket size is rising — this is late-cycle behaviour. Affluent investors are entering via SIPs, not mass retail. This suggests the retail equity bull market is maturing. Reduce exposure to mass-market consumption plays (FMCG, retail) and rotate into quality, dividend-paying stocks. Avoid new equity fund launches (NFO collections at 6th percentile). MF AUM at 80th percentile suggests valuations are stretched; take profits on momentum plays. Monitor SIP account growth — if it turns negative, retail capitulation is near.","pct_10y":85,"pct_note":"SIP accounts at 86th percentile (104.5M) and SIP AUM at 82nd percentile (₹16.64L crore) are at multi-year highs. Rising average ticket size amid slowing growth is a classic late-cycle signal that has preceded 15-20% equity corrections in the past.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:4: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":4,"signal_theme":"OIL / COMMODITY RISK","status":"risk","title":"Oil Spike Threatens CAD — Brent at 82nd Percentile","data_text":"Brent crude at $98.66/bbl (up from $94.75 prior month) — 82nd percentile. WTI at $99.43/bbl (82nd percentile). Natural gas at $2.735/mmBtu (16th percentile, but up from $2.724). Iron ore at $161.91/tonne (63rd percentile, up from $107.83 — a 50% spike). Copper down to $5.698/tonne (93rd percentile, but declining) — a divergence suggesting demand concerns. India's oil import bill will rise ~$4-5 billion annually at current prices; CAD pressure is building.","implication":"Oil at 82nd percentile is a material headwind for India's current account. At $100/bbl, India's annual oil import bill rises by $5-6 billion, widening CAD by 0.3-0.4% of GDP. This constrains RBI's ability to cut rates aggressively and pressures INR. Hedge oil exposure via energy stocks (ONGC, Reliance) — they benefit from higher crude. Avoid import-heavy sectors (chemicals, fertilisers, airlines) until oil retreats to $85-90. Iron ore spike is China-driven (stimulus hopes); monitor for reversal. Copper weakness despite high percentile is a demand warning — reduce cyclical exposure.","pct_10y":82,"pct_note":"Brent at 82nd percentile has historically preceded 2-3% INR depreciation and 50-75bps of RBI rate-cut delays. Oil above $100/bbl is a structural CAD risk for India.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:5: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":5,"signal_theme":"GLOBAL LIQUIDITY","status":"watch","title":"US Rates Sticky — Fed Balance Sheet Expanding","data_text":"US 10Y Treasury at 4.26% (flat from 4.34% prior month). Fed funds rate at 3.625% (unchanged). Fed balance sheet at $6.675 trillion (up from $6.657 trillion) — expanding despite rate stability. US CPI at 2.4% (flat), core PCE at 3.06% (down from 3.1%) — disinflation stalling. US GDP SAAR at 0.7% (down from 1.4%) — growth decelerating sharply. DXY at 99.04 (up from 98.82) — dollar strength persisting. S&P 500 at 6,782.81 (96th percentile), Nasdaq at 22,634 (100th percentile) — valuations at extremes despite growth deceleration.","implication":"The Fed is in a bind: growth is slowing (0.7% SAAR), but inflation is sticky (core PCE at 3.06%). Balance sheet expansion without rate cuts signals QE-lite — liquidity is being injected to support equity valuations, not growth. This is a carry-trade setup: cheap money in the US, but rates not falling. DXY strength pressures EM currencies (including INR) and makes US assets expensive. Reduce US equity exposure (S&P 500 at 96th percentile, Nasdaq at 100th) — valuations don't justify growth deceleration. Overweight India equities as a relative value play, but hedge INR depreciation risk. Monitor Fed balance sheet — if it accelerates, expect a liquidity-driven rally; if it stalls, expect a correction.","pct_10y":67,"pct_note":"Fed balance sheet at 46th percentile but expanding; this is unusual in a stable-rate environment. Historically, balance sheet expansion without rate cuts has preceded 10-15% equity corrections within 6 months.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:6: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":6,"signal_theme":"INR / FX RESERVES","status":"watch","title":"INR at 84th Percentile — FII Outflows Accelerating","data_text":"INR/USD at 92.59 (84th percentile — near 10-year highs). FII equity net outflows at -₹8,692 crore (Feb 2026, down from -₹3,295 crore prior month) — outflows accelerating 2.6x month-on-month. DII equity net inflows at ₹7,979 crore (down from ₹8,593 crore) — domestic support weakening. DXY at 99.04 (46th percentile, but rising) — dollar strength is a headwind. RBI's easing cycle (repo at 5.25%) is not offsetting carry-trade unwind and FII selling.","implication":"INR weakness is accelerating despite RBI rate cuts — this is a structural FII outflow story, not a cyclical one. FII outflows of ₹8,692 crore in a single month are material; if sustained, they'll force RBI to intervene or hike rates. INR at 92.59 is near the 84th percentile; a break above 93 would signal a loss of RBI control. Domestic flows (DIIs) are slowing — they can't absorb FII selling indefinitely. Reduce INR exposure; hedge via USD forwards or short INR positions. Avoid import-sensitive sectors (IT services, pharma) until INR stabilises. RBI will likely pause rate cuts to defend INR — this is a headwind for duration and equities. Monitor FII flows weekly; if outflows exceed ₹10,000 crore/month, expect RBI intervention or a 50bps rate hike.","pct_10y":84,"pct_note":"INR at 84th percentile (92.59) is near 10-year weakness. Historically, INR at this level has preceded 2-3% further depreciation and RBI tightening cycles that have lasted 4-6 months.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:7: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Office Absorption Surge Decouples From IT Stocks","data_text":"Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft (up from 28.2 mn sqft prior month) — 24th percentile but accelerating. Office vacancy at 13.9% (down from 17.2% prior month) — a 330bps compression in 1 month. Nifty IT index flat YoY despite office absorption surge. Rent in Bengaluru at ₹100.6/sqft (up from ₹91.8) — a 9.5% spike. However, Nifty IT services (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) are trading at 18-20x FY27E earnings with flat growth. Global PMI composite at 53.2 (81st percentile) signals strong global demand, yet IT stocks are not participating. This divergence suggests GCCs (Global Capability Centres) and non-IT corporates are the office absorption drivers, not traditional IT services.","implication":"The office real estate recovery is NOT a proxy for IT services growth. GCCs (Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Apple) and financial services firms are absorbing office space in Bengaluru and Mumbai, not IT services companies. This means: (1) Avoid Nifty IT on the assumption of office-driven growth — IT services are in structural decline (offshoring, automation). (2) Overweight commercial real estate plays (Embassy REIT at ₹437.42, up from ₹433.75) and office landlords — they're the true beneficiaries. (3) The office vacancy compression is a GCC story, not an IT story. (4) Rent inflation in Bengaluru (+9.5%) will pressure IT services margins further — they're paying more for space while revenues stagnate. Reduce IT services exposure; rotate into REITs and GCC-adjacent plays (IT infrastructure, data centres). This is a 2-3 year structural headwind for traditional IT services.","pct_10y":24,"pct_note":"Office absorption at 24th percentile is historically low, but the 330bps vacancy compression in 1 month is unprecedented. This rapid tightening, combined with flat IT stock performance, suggests a structural shift in office demand drivers — from IT services to GCCs and non-IT corporates. This divergence has preceded 15-20% underperformance of IT services stocks vs. broader indices in the past.","is_surprise":true}

executive_summary.2026-04-08:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

GDP 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 puts India in unambiguous expansion — 3 of 6 macro dimensions are in growth mode simultaneously. GST collections at ₹2,00,064 Cr YoY alongside PV sales growth of 10.5% confirm demand is broad-based, not sector-specific. CPI at 3.21% with fuel deflation at -3.78% gives RBI room to hold the repo at 5.25% without triggering a policy reversal — duration trades remain intact. Credit growth at 14.3% against deposit growth of 10.9% — CD ratio now 83.04% — is the single structural fault line in an otherwise clean expansion print.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

S&P 500 at 6,779 (+2.5%) and Nasdaq at 22,653 (+3.0%, 100th percentile) signal a global risk-on regime that is not priced for disruption. Brent at $94.98/bbl — down 13% from $109.27 — compresses India's import bill and is the single most important tailwind for CAD management this quarter. Copper at $5.766/lb (95th percentile, 10-year range) and iron ore surging to $161.91/t from $108.32 signal industrial demand acceleration, particularly out of China — this is not a soft-landing signal, it is a re-acceleration signal. DXY at 98.84 and US 10Y at 4.246% — both softening — remove the twin headwinds of dollar strength and rate pressure that dominated 2024.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr on 9.92 Cr accounts is structural bedrock — a -3.7% MoM dip in SIP inflows to ₹29,845 Cr is noise against 15% YoY growth. Equity MF net inflows accelerated to ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr), and NFO collections exploded to ₹3,955 Cr from ₹806 Cr — DII buying is not slowing, it is rotating into new paper. India VIX collapsing 20.25% to 19.70 from 24.7 — concurrent with US VIX falling to 21.25 — signals a global liquidity unlock that historically precedes 6–8 weeks of sustained equity inflows. FII positioning remains the swing variable; INR stability at 92.59 against a weakening DXY (98.84) removes the currency hedge cost that was suppressing foreign allocation.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty50 at 23,997 (+3.78%) and Sensex at 77,563 (+3.95%) are pricing the base case — not the bull case — which means reform acceleration or a credit resolution catalyst could drive a further 8–12% re-rating. Real estate presents a structural contradiction: home loan disbursements at ₹5,50,000 Cr are strong, avg rates fell 50 bps to 7.65%, yet RE sales declined 6.7% to 1,01,675 units against an affordability index deteriorating 32% to 0.61 — volume is being bought by rate cuts, not by income growth. Office REITs are the cleaner play: absorption at 83.3 (+13.7%) with vacancy compressing to 15.2% — Embassy REIT at ₹429.85 and Mindspace at ₹449.98 are at 75–79th percentiles, not stretched. Unsold residential inventory at 6,00,000 units caps developer pricing power → residential equity upside is limited without an affordability shock reversal.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: #1 — CD ratio at 83.04% with NBFC credit growing 15–17% on constrained deposits → funding cost spike → NBFC NIM compression → credit crunch cascade (bear scenario trigger, high probability, systemic impact). #2 — Affordability index at 0.61 (+32% deterioration) against 6,00,000 units unsold inventory; any rate reversal → mortgage stress → residential developer defaults (medium probability, concentrated impact). #3 — Copper at 95th percentile and iron ore at $161.91/t signal commodity re-inflation risk; Brent reverting toward $110 — CAD widens $25bn annually → INR pressure → RBI forced tightening → equity multiple compression. Watch the CD ratio print for April — at 83.04%, a further 50–100 bps widening triggers mandatory RBI macro-prudential intervention.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP with PMI composite at 56.5 is the headline — but core sector growth at 2.3% (down from 4.7%) and capacity utilisation slipping to 74.8% are the footnotes that matter more. GST at ₹2.0 lakh crore and two-wheeler sales at +13.4% confirm that consumption is genuinely broad-based — rural demand is not a laggard this cycle. What breaks this narrative: food CPI accelerating to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month, with the FAO food index at 128.5 and Brent at $98.66 — the RBI's 4.6% inflation forecast revision (up from 2.1%) suggests the institution already sees the trapdoor. India's growth is real; its durability over the next two quarters is not.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP SAAR at 0.7% — halved from 1.4% prior quarter — while S&P 500 sits at the 96th percentile and Nasdaq at the 100th percentile is the most dangerous valuation-growth divergence in global markets today. Core PCE at 3.06% with the Fed funds rate at 3.625% means the Fed is already cutting into above-target inflation — a policy error in slow motion — while the Fed balance sheet expanding to $6.675 trillion signals stealth liquidity injection. China's composite PMI recovering to 50.25 from 49.25 and iron ore spiking 50% to $161.91/tonne suggest Beijing stimulus is landing in commodities, not consumption — a pattern that exports inflation to India's import bill. Copper declining to $5.698/tonne while iron ore surges is the demand-quality warning: infrastructure spending without industrial demand recovery.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

FII equity outflows at -₹8,692 crore — accelerating 2.6x from -₹3,295 crore prior month — are not being fully absorbed by DIIs at +₹7,979 crore (itself declining from ₹8,593 crore), leaving a net liquidity deficit in Indian equities. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore are declining from ₹31,002 crore, and NFO collections at ₹1,939 crore (6th percentile) signal that new retail investor acquisition has stalled — the SIP engine is running on existing accounts, not fresh capital. INR at 92.59 (84th percentile) with DXY rising to 99.04 means RBI is either burning reserves to defend the currency or accepting depreciation — both outcomes compress the real rate advantage that justified the easing cycle. Average SIP ticket size rising to ₹15,900 confirms affluent money is entering even as mass-market participation plateaus — a bifurcation that historically precedes index concentration risk.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty at 23,775 (76th percentile) with Bank Nifty at 54,821 (90th percentile) — the banking index is priced for perfection at a moment when CD ratio at 83.04% and deposit growth at 10.8% structurally constrain NIM expansion. Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft with vacancy compressing 330bps to 13.9% and Bengaluru rents spiking 9.5% to ₹100.6/sqft is a genuine real estate signal — but Nifty IT flat YoY confirms GCCs and non-IT corporates are the absorption driver, not traditional IT services at 18-20x FY27E with flat growth. Residential real estate affordability improving to 0.61 from 0.46 with home loan rates at 8.15% is structurally positive — but sales units declining to 101,675 from 108,970 while launches rise to 126,265 means unsold inventory at 600,000 units is building, not clearing. Embassy REIT at ₹437 (78th percentile) is the cleanest expression of the office absorption thesis — own the physical asset, not the IT services P&L.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, Brent at $98.66 (82nd percentile) — every $10 adds ~$15bn to India's annual import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — imported inflation feeds CPI with a two-quarter lag — RBI forced to pause or reverse the easing cycle — corporate capex IRRs deteriorate — Nifty derates. Second, the CD ratio at 83.04% widening further — deposit rate wars → NIM compression → Bank Nifty earnings downgrades → passive outflows from index funds that are 30%+ weighted to financials. Third, US growth at 0.7% SAAR with equities at the 96th-100th percentile — a mean-reversion in S&P 500 triggers risk-off globally — FII outflows from India accelerate beyond the -₹8,692 crore February run-rate — INR breaks above 94. Watch Friday's RBI MPC minutes for any language shift on the inflation forecast revision from 2.1% to 4.6% — that 250bps internal revision is the single most important data point this week.

"}
